## [00:00 - 03:02]

Hi, this is Don Crowther with a video about handling questions as you go along. So my best description of an ideal webinar is a controlled conversation, meaning an ongoing fluid exchange between you and your audience, controlled as in you control it, but you're also helping your audience to engage. So we've already talked about chats and polls and how that works. I acknowledge it. Sometimes I don't. Sometimes I'm just going through and I'm teaching what I'm teaching and I just make it sound like that was a natural part of the teaching that came up at that particular point. Or sometimes I will acknowledge it. I play it by ear as I'm going along. And so I suggest you learn to keep your eye on the chat and play along with your audience, listening along comments as they that they make as they're appropriate. And by rule, I do like to mention them by name, but I don't do it all the time because remember I started off by asking them to hold their questions until the end. And so if I start using names every time I talk about something, it encourages people to put them in and that then I start to lose control. And so I tend to balance this out remembering, hey, I'm the guy who's in control, at least theoretically, may not actually be true, but I'm supposed to be in control. So here's what's going to happen. People are going to ask questions and I suggest you handle them as you go along if they fit. And if one goes comes along that you're going to handle later on, you can acknowledge it. You can ignore it. Or sometimes you can acknowledge it and tell them that you'll be talking about that later on. By the way, it's good to have a staff member online to answer questions as they come in. We'll talk more about that in a separate video. And by the way, your your audience may also do answering. What I have found is over time, I've developed this loyal cadre of people who attend my webinars who many of them know the answers to questions and they'll get on chat and just answer people's questions for me. And I'm going, hey, this is really cool. So again, remember, you're the one in control. And so part of that being in control is don't get let yourself get pulled down if someone is chatting in a distracting way.

## [03:02 - 06:01]

So those distractions could be everything from challenging something that you that you just said to, hey, how about them packers to taking some small piece and running some small comment that you made or maybe even someone else made as a comment and running off on that. And pretty soon they're talking about something that isn't even close to what you're talking about. So you got several options when this happens. Probably the biggest most important one is just ignore them. Some webinar services allow you to be able to hide chat at a certain point. So as you're going along through the webinar and if if the if the chat is becoming a negative thing, a distraction, it's turned against you. It's troll written territory. You sometimes can just turn off chat. And by the way, don't be afraid and give permission to your staff to kick out a troll and to just keep doing it over and over again. If they come back in under different under different sign ins and you may even have to close out the chat in some situations. I've had a couple of webinars where I just had somebody got on who was a hater and they just wanted to hate. And most of the time, in fact, I can only think of one of those where they were hating me. They're hating and ridiculing other people in the chat. And it's like I can take it when you hate on me. But if you're going to hate on my staff or worse, my audience. Bam, you're out of here. So sometimes you'll get somebody who asks a repeat question where they'll ask a question. They don't get the answer. So they'll cut and paste it back in the chat two minutes later. And then two minutes later, they do it again and they keep doing it. And it really gets annoying not only because doesn't annoy everyone in the audience, but it starts making them think, oh, they're avoiding answering this question. And so when that happens, sometimes I or I will have my staff jump in and tell them, hey, Janice, that question you're asking, I appreciate it. I'm going to answer that question in the Q&A portion of this webinar later on. Now, remember, given that and in spite of that, you don't have to answer every question you get asked. Realize when you listen to politicians and they're asked a question, they almost never actually answer the questions that they're given. It's just not one that should be directly answered because it won't help people to be count to be in the frame of mind they need to be to buy your product. ## [06:02 - 07:13]

You can redirect a question by saying what you really want to be asking is this. Just realize they don't control your webinar. You're in control. Remember, engagement is a key differentiating factor between you and your competitors. And don't worry if this is hard for you. You're going to get better at it over time.
